questions.questionText: On a lateral neck X-ray in a child with stridor, the 'steeple sign' (subglottic narrowing of the trachea) is characteristic of:
questions.optionB: Croup (laryngotracheobronchitis — viral, subglottic edema causing symmetrical narrowing → steeple/pencil-point sign on AP neck XR)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Steeple sign = subglottic narrowing on AP neck XR = croup. Why correct: Croup (parainfluenza virus) → subglottic edema → loss of normal 'shouldered' subglottic shape → symmetric tapering = steeple/pencil-point sign on AP view. Why A wrong: Epiglottitis → 'thumbprint sign' on lateral neck XR (swollen epiglottis). Drooling, toxic child. Why C wrong: Foreign body — asymmetric narrowing, may be radiopaque. Expiratory films for air trapping. Why D wrong: Retropharyngeal abscess — widened prevertebral soft tissue on lateral neck XR (>7mm at C2, >14mm at C6 in children). Clinical relevance: Croup: barking cough, inspiratory stridor, worse at night, 6 months-3 years. Treatment: nebulized epinephrine, dexamethasone. Exam trap: Steeple = croup (AP view); Thumbprint = epiglottitis (lateral view). Memory aid: 'Croup = Church steeple (tapering); Epiglottitis = thumb (swollen).' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – X-Ray.
questions.conceptTag: Steeple sign croup subglottic thumbprint epiglottitis

questions.questionText: On abdominal X-ray, 'lead-pipe' appearance of the colon (loss of haustral folds) with shortening is characteristic of:
questions.optionB: Ulcerative colitis (chronic mucosal inflammation → loss of haustra → smooth, narrowed, shortened 'lead-pipe' colon on barium enema/XR)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Loss of haustra + lead-pipe colon = chronic UC. Why correct: UC — chronic mucosal inflammation (superficial) → mucosal edema → pseudopolyps → progressive loss of haustral folds → featureless 'lead-pipe' colon. Starts from rectum, extends proximally. Why A wrong: Crohn's — skip lesions, 'string sign' of terminal ileum (strictures), cobblestoning, fistulae. Why C wrong: IBS — no structural changes on imaging. Why D wrong: Diverticular disease — outpouchings in sigmoid, no loss of haustra. Clinical relevance: UC on barium enema: lead-pipe, pseudopolyps, backwash ileitis. Toxic megacolon: colon dilation >6cm (surgical emergency). Exam trap: Lead-pipe = UC; String sign = Crohn's. Know these classic barium enema findings. Memory aid: 'UC = Uniformly smooth Colon (lead-pipe = no folds).' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – X-Ray.
questions.conceptTag: UC lead-pipe colon loss haustra barium

questions.questionText: A child swallowed a coin. On AP chest X-ray, the coin appears as a circular opacity in the esophagus. On lateral view, the coin is seen edge-on. Where is the coin lodged?
questions.optionB: Esophagus (coins in esophagus appear en face/circular on AP and edge-on on lateral; tracheal coins are opposite — edge-on on AP, en face on lateral)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Esophageal coin = circular on AP, edge-on on lateral. Tracheal coin = opposite. Why correct: Esophagus is in the coronal plane → coin lies flat → appears circular on AP. Trachea is in the sagittal plane → coin in trachea lies sagittally → edge-on on AP, circular on lateral. Why A wrong: Tracheal coin is edge-on on AP (opposite pattern). Why C wrong: Bronchial FB → asymmetric air trapping on expiratory film. Why D wrong: Stomach — coin below diaphragm. Clinical relevance: Most common site of esophageal FB impaction: cricopharyngeus (C6, upper esophageal sphincter). Button batteries → emergency removal (chemical burns). Exam trap: AP circular = esophagus; AP edge-on = trachea. This is CLASSIC exam material. Memory aid: 'Esophagus lies in coronal plane → coin lies flat on AP → Circular.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – X-Ray.
questions.conceptTag: Esophageal coin AP circular lateral edge-on

questions.questionText: On X-ray, 'sail sign' in the anterior mediastinum of a young adult is suggestive of:
questions.optionB: Thymic enlargement/thymoma (thymus has triangular 'sail' shape on AP CXR in children; thymic mass in adults — anterior mediastinal mass)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Sail sign = thymic shadow in anterior mediastinum. Why correct: Normal thymus in children appears as 'thymic sail' on CXR — triangular opacity overlying heart shadow. In adults, anterior mediastinal mass with sail-like morphology → thymoma. Anterior mediastinal masses: '4 T's' — Thymoma, Teratoma, Terrible lymphoma, Thyroid. Why A wrong: Lymphoma is also anterior mediastinal but typically lobulated mass, no sail sign. Why C wrong: Aortic aneurysm — middle mediastinum, widened mediastinum. Why D wrong: Pneumomediastinum — air outlining mediastinal structures (continuous diaphragm sign, ring-around-the-artery sign). Clinical relevance: Thymoma associated with myasthenia gravis (30-50%). CT for evaluation. Exam trap: Normal thymic sail in children ≤3 years is physiological — don't misdiagnose. Thymic wave sign = scalloped border due to ribs = normal. Memory aid: 'Sail sign = thymus Sailing in anterior mediastinum.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – X-Ray.
questions.conceptTag: Sail sign thymus thymoma anterior mediastinum 4T

questions.questionText: Barium swallow showing a 'bird-beak' or 'rat-tail' appearance of the distal esophagus with proximal dilatation is diagnostic of:
questions.optionB: Achalasia cardia (failure of LES relaxation + absent peristalsis → dilated esophagus tapering smoothly at cardia)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Bird-beak/rat-tail at GEJ + dilated esophagus = achalasia. Why correct: Achalasia — absent myenteric plexus (Auerbach's) → failed LES relaxation + absent peristalsis. Barium swallow: dilated esophagus with smooth, symmetric, tapering at GEJ (bird-beak). Retained food/barium. Why A wrong: Esophageal carcinoma — irregular, asymmetric narrowing ('apple-core'/'rat-tail' but irregular margins vs smooth in achalasia). Why C wrong: Zenker's — posterior pharyngeal pouch (not distal esophagus). Why D wrong: GERD stricture — smooth but SHORT narrowing, not bird-beak. Clinical relevance: Manometry = gold standard for diagnosis. Treatment: Heller myotomy or pneumatic dilatation. Exam trap: Smooth tapering = achalasia; Irregular tapering = carcinoma. Surface margins differentiate. Memory aid: 'Achalasia = smooth bird-Beak tapering; cArcinoma = irregular Asymmetric.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – X-Ray.
